title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Superintendent Spence discuss the murder of thirteen-year-old Joyce Reynolds. Poirot describes the cruelty of the killing method, which involved a trick related to biting an apple, suggesting the perpetrator might have a history of child abuse. Spence observes that the local community is unstable due to high population turnover, though he notes long-term residents such as Miss Emery and Dr. Ferguson. Regarding potential suspects from the party's age group, Spence identifies Nicholas Lanchester, a young man of seventeen or eighteen, and a person named Desmond, who had previously been held in custody following a psychological report. Spence also considers the possibility that an outsider entered the premises through an open door or window.

The investigation centers on a claim made by Joyce prior to her death. Poirot recounts that during a conversation regarding Mrs. Oliver’s books, Joyce mentioned having witnessed a murder once. While Mrs. Oliver and Spence initially dismissed this as an exaggeration, Poirot argues that Joyce's death shortly after making the claim suggests a causal link. He posits that if Joyce had witnessed a crime years earlier—perhaps an accident she did not realize was intentional—the discussion of murder may have triggered her memory, prompting the killer to act to protect their secret. Spence notes that the party included approximately fourteen children and various adults, including Dr. Ferguson and the parish priest. Poirot suggests the murderer may be someone who appears unremarkable and is currently acting out of shock because they have been discovered.
